input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation relies on a range of Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) tools and languages.  At the design phase, SysML v1.7 is used to capture functional and logical architectures, with Integrations drawn using SysML Interaction Blocks (IBDs) and state‑machine diagrams for behavior.  The component & connector model is described with a subset of the ACME architecture description language, and for formal behavior modeling MontiArc Automaton is employed, which can be translated into state‑machine representations for simulation.  Simulation and numerical analysis are performed with MathWorks Simulink, enabling co‑simulation with embedded code generators.  The resulting models are assembled into code or script in general‑purpose languages such as C++ and Java, which are compiled and deployed to edge or cloud platforms to host the twin.  No dedicated database or ontology platform is mentioned in the source document, but the modeling workflow implies the use of a repository (e.g., SPARQL compatible or relational) to store design artifacts and simulation results.  Overall, the toolkit covers SysML, ACME, MontiArc, Simulink, C++/Java, and standard MBSE repositories as enablers for developing the Digital Twin.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin computational models are outlined through MBSE practices, employing SysML artifacts for structural and functional architecture, MontiArc Automaton or SysML state‑machine constructs for behavioral representation, and Simulink simulations for dynamic analysis. Data inputs are derived from model‑based specifications, mathematical simulation outputs, and code artifacts in C++ or Java. Model relationships are expressed via component‑connector topologies defined in ADLs such as ACME, AADL or MontiArc, enabling hierarchical composition and interaction mapping. In the DT constellation these models serve to map physical state variables to virtual entities, support predictive simulation, and form the basis for real‑time monitoring and control interfaces.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin’s life‑cycle is derived from a multi‑level Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) approach: 1) *Requirements* are captured in a requirements repository. 2) *Functional architecture* is modelled with SysML, defining system functions and high‑level behaviour. 3) *Logical architecture* is expressed in architecture description languages such as MontiArc, AADL, ACME or SysML IBDs, representing components, connectors, interfaces and interrelations. 4) *Product/technical layer* implements the logical plan using simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) or general‑purpose code in C++ or Java. Transitions between phases are managed through model‑to‑model transformations and versioned model repositories, while governance is exercised by the engineering team at each release, ensuring traceability from requirements to executable artifacts.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Digital Twin engineering follows MBSE methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES and SMArDT. System structure is captured using SysML and architecture description languages (ACME, AADL, MontiArc). Behavioural aspects are further refined with state‐machine models in SysML v1.7 or MontiArc Automaton extensions. Simulation and physical data integration are achieved with MathWorks Simulink, additional simulation tools, and by generating executable artefacts in C++ or Java. Multiple modelling notations are coordinated through model‑to‑model transformation pipelines, enabling the evolution of the Twin from abstract logical models to concrete technical implementations. Quality assurance, testing and version control are implied by the use of standard MBSE toolchains, although explicit procedures are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin architecture is compliant with standard modelling languages: SysML (ISO/IEC 19510), ACME, AADL, MontiArc and SysML IBDs. These standards provide structural, behavioural and inter‑connectivity specifications, enabling interoperability with other MBSE tools and facilitating integration into industry processes. No explicit compliance frameworks or certification requirements are mentioned in the document.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
